ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Indian-style crispy corn; closest parent is corn snack food product (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Corn-based snacks) used as closest parent; no exact code for Indian masala crispy corn exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific snack preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of crispy corn is approximately 150 g, presented as a bowl of golden fried corn kernels seasoned with spices.

nutrient_source.title: Corn, yellow, sweet, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with deep-frying oil absorption and spice seasoning estimates from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for comparable fried corn snack preparations
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Crispy corn is not listed as a discrete entry in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for sweet corn kernels (FDC ID 169998) with standard deep-frying oil absorption factors (~8–10 g fat per 100 g product) and spice seasoning contributions as documented in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for comparable fried corn snack preparations. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; corn-based snack / fried corn kernel analogous entry; GI ~52 for corn-based fried snack products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian-style crispy corn. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for corn-based fried snack products and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables for comparable corn snack preparations. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 50.2 g carbohydrate × 1.50 serving size) / 100 = 39.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from salt and chaat masala seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit crispy corn intake due to its high sodium content; a 150 g serving contributes approximately 630 mg sodium, representing over 27% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 52) combined with high carbohydrate density and large portion sizes elevating glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of crispy corn is in the low-to-moderate range (GI 52), the high carbohydrate content and typical restaurant portion size produce a high glycemic load (~39 per serving), making portion control essential for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat from deep-frying with relatively low saturated fat; high sodium is an additional cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crispy corn is deep-fried and carries a moderate fat load (~18.5 g per 100 g); while saturated fat is relatively low (~2.1 g per 100 g), the combination of high sodium and caloric density warrants infrequent consumption for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Corn is a popular Indian snack made from sweet corn kernels that are coated in a seasoned batter of cornflour and rice flour, then deep-fried until golden and crunchy. The kernels are tossed with a blend of spices including chilli powder, cumin, chaat masala, and salt, producing a bold, tangy, and spicy flavour profile with a satisfying crunch. The dish is widely served as a starter or street-food snack across India and has become a fixture on Indian restaurant menus in Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, crispy corn is energy-dense (~390 kcal per 100 g) due to deep-frying, with a moderate protein content (~7.2 g), significant carbohydrate load (~50.2 g), and elevated sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) from spice seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low-moderate), but the glycemic load per restaurant serving is high (~39), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable. It is not recommended for individuals on sodium-restricted diets due to its high salt content from seasoning blends.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy corn?
Is crispy corn good for diabetics?
What spices are used in crispy corn?

confidence_reason: Crispy corn is not listed as a discrete entry in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy sweet corn data combined with standard deep-frying oil absorption factors and ICMR-NIN spice seasoning references. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable corn-based fried snack products. Medium confidence reflects the estimation basis rather than a direct source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated; no discrete approved-source entry exists for Indian-style crispy corn. Values derived from USDA FDC SR Legacy (sweet corn, FDC ID 169998) plus deep-frying oil absorption (~8–10 g fat per 100 g) and ICMR-NIN spice seasoning data. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for sweet corn and is not expected to be present in this fried corn snack. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food type. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 and vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0; these are confirmed zero as crispy corn is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients, confirmed by the USDA SR Legacy corn entry and ICMR-NIN plant food data. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as the dish is prepared with standard vegetable oil without hydrogenation. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database corn-based fried snack entries; no direct measurement for this specific preparation exists. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 50.2 × 1.50) / 100 = 39.2 for a 150 g restaurant serving. 7. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact ontology entry exists for Indian masala crispy corn. 8. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded as it does not describe this food.

improvement_actions_taken: Added full multilingual local_names for Indian cuisine in Singapore context (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Estimated nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy + ICMR-NIN deep-frying and spice seasoning data.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-absence justification in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, trans_fat_g) as confirmed zero for plant-based food.
Set GI to 52 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database), gi_category to 'low' per ≤55 threshold rule.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving as 39.2 for 150 g serving.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Ensured aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent — all language-specific strings verified in flat list.
Disregarded 'Butter Chicken' cuisine_tag from input as platform artefact.
Set ontology match_type values to only allowed strings: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
